title: Data Security in Digital Health: How Able Assess Protects Patient Data
description: HIPAA, GDPR, and ISO 27001 compliance. Encryption standards, security architecture, and how Able Assess safeguards patient data.

blocks.question: How do digital falls screening tools protect patient data?
blocks.directAnswer: Platforms like Able Assess use encryption (AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.2+ in transit), role-based access control, multi-factor authentication, and regular security audits. Compliance with HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), and ISO 27001 certification ensures patient data is protected against unauthorized access and breaches.

tldrBullets.text: Encryption: AES-256 for stored data, TLS 1.2+ for data in transit, protecting against interception
Access control: role-based permissions ensure only authorized clinicians view patient data
Compliance: HIPAA, GDPR, and ISO 27001 certification demonstrate third-party validated security practices

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Patient data is the most sensitive asset in healthcare. Assessment results include names, dates of birth, medical information, and functional test results. If this data is breached, patients face identity theft, privacy violation, and loss of trust. Digital tools handling this data must protect it rigorously.

Data security operates on three levels: encryption (transforming data into unreadable form), access control (restricting who can view data), and compliance (ensuring systems meet regulatory standards). Able Assess implements all three.

Encryption is fundamental. Data at rest (stored in databases) is encrypted using AES-256, a military-grade standard. This means if a server is stolen, the data on it is useless without the decryption key. Data in transit (moving between the assessment device and cloud servers, or between the assessment tool and EHR) is encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher. This prevents eavesdropping on network traffic.

HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) is the US legal standard. It requires healthcare organizations to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for electronic protected health information (ePHI). GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is the EU standard governing any personal data, including health data. ISO 27001 is an international certification for information security management. These three standards overlap; compliance with one often satisfies parts of the others.

blocks.body:
Why data security matters in falls screening

Falls assessment data is sensitive. It reveals functional limitations, fall risk, and sometimes underlying cognitive or balance disorders. If breached, this information could be misused: insurers denying coverage based on fall risk, employers discriminating against employees with balance disorders, or criminals targeting individuals at home who they know have mobility issues.

Healthcare data breaches are costly. The average cost of a healthcare data breach is USD 10.93M (2023), the highest of any industry. Costs include notification, regulatory fines (HIPAA penalties up to USD 1.5M), remediation, and reputational damage. Beyond cost, breaches erode patient trust and jeopardize your organization's reputation.

Regulatory obligations are strict. If you use digital tools handling HIPAA-covered data (in the US), you are legally liable for the security practices of your vendors. If Able Assess has a breach and patient data is exposed, the liability falls on your organization, not just the vendor. This is why vendor security practices matter critically: your compliance depends on them.

Digital health tools also face unique threats. Unlike paper records (which require physical theft), digital data can be accessed remotely. Hackers can attempt to breach cloud servers, intercept network traffic, or exploit software vulnerabilities. Robust security practices defend against these threats.

Regulatory requirements: HIPAA, GDPR, and compliance

HIPAA (US): The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act is the primary US healthcare privacy and security law. HIPAA applies to covered entities (healthcare providers, health plans, healthcare clearinghouses) and business associates (vendors handling covered data on behalf of covered entities). If you use Able Assess in the US, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) must be in place specifying that Able Assess agrees to HIPAA requirements.

HIPAA requires: (1) Administrative safeguards (workforce security, access controls, security awareness training), (2) Physical safeguards (facility access controls, workstation security), (3) Technical safeguards (access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, transmission security). Violation penalties are USD 100-USD 50,000 per violation per day, with annual caps reaching millions.

GDPR (EU/UK): The General Data Protection Regulation governs personal data processing for any individual in the EU or UK. It applies regardless of where your organization is located. If you collect assessment data from EU/UK residents, GDPR applies. GDPR requires data minimization (collect only necessary data), purpose limitation (use data only for stated purposes), and security (implement technical and organizational measures).

GDPR gives individuals rights: access to their data, correction of inaccurate data, deletion ("right to be forgotten"), and data portability (receiving their data in transferable format). GDPR violations incur penalties up to EUR 20M or 4% of global annual revenue, whichever is higher. Vendors must have Data Processing Agreements (DPA) specifying roles and responsibilities.

ISO 27001: This is an international information security management standard, not legally mandated but increasingly expected. ISO 27001 certification means the organization has implemented a comprehensive information security management system (ISMS) and undergoes annual independent audits. Certification demonstrates that security practices are not ad-hoc but systematic and regularly validated.

Security architecture: Encryption, access control, and authentication

Data Encryption: Able Assess encrypts patient data at rest and in transit. Data at rest (stored in databases) uses AES-256 encryption. AES is the Advanced Encryption Standard, approved by the US National Institute of Standards and Technology. 256-bit encryption (key length of 256 bits) is military-grade security, providing protection against brute-force attacks. Even if a server is stolen and its hard drive accessed, the data is encrypted and useless without the decryption key.

Data in transit uses TLS 1.2 or higher (Transport Layer Security). TLS is the protocol that secures HTTPS connections. When you visit a website with a padlock icon, TLS is protecting your communication. TLS 1.2+ is the current standard. Older versions (SSL, TLS 1.0/1.1) have known vulnerabilities and should not be used for healthcare data.

Key Management: Encryption keys must be protected separately from encrypted data. If keys are stored alongside encrypted data, encryption provides no protection. Able Assess uses a separate key management system: encryption keys are stored on dedicated, access-restricted servers. Key rotation occurs regularly (every 90 days typical), meaning old keys are retired and new keys generated.

Access Control: Even with encryption, systems must restrict who can access patient data. Able Assess implements role-based access control (RBAC). Users are assigned roles (clinician, administrator, auditor) with corresponding permissions. A clinician can view assessment results for their patients but cannot see all patient data in the system. An administrator can configure accounts but might not access patient-specific data. This principle of least privilege ensures users access only data needed for their job.

Authentication: Users prove their identity before accessing data. Able Assess supports multi-factor authentication (MFA): users enter username/password (something you know) plus a one-time code from an authenticator app (something you have). MFA prevents account compromise even if passwords are stolen.

Audit Logging: Every access to patient data is logged. Logs record: who accessed data, when, what data was accessed, and what action was taken. These audit logs are reviewed regularly for suspicious activity. If unauthorized access is detected, it can be traced and investigated. Audit logs support HIPAA and GDPR compliance.

How Able Assess implements security measures

Able Assess is built on a secure cloud infrastructure (AWS GovCloud or equivalent compliant regions for healthcare). Servers are hosted in isolated virtual environments with network segmentation: databases are not directly connected to user-facing applications, reducing attack surface.

All data is encrypted: in transit using TLS 1.2+, at rest using AES-256. Encryption keys are stored separately in a key management service. Access is controlled via RBAC; users see only data relevant to their role and patients. Multi-factor authentication is available for all accounts, with optional enforcement.

The platform undergoes regular security testing: vulnerability scanning (automated checks for known security flaws), penetration testing (ethical hackers attempt to breach the system), and code review (security experts examine code for vulnerabilities). Any vulnerabilities discovered are remediated promptly and communicated to customers.

Able Assess maintains ISO 27001 certification, with annual audits by independent auditors. HIPAA Business Associate Agreements are in place for US customers. GDPR Data Processing Agreements are in place for EU/UK customers. Security documentation is available for customers to review during vendor selection or audits.

Incident response procedures are documented. If a security breach occurs (unlikely but possible), Able Assess notifies affected customers within 24 hours and provides guidance on customer obligations (notifying individuals, regulators, etc.).

blocks.heading: What the evidence says: Healthcare data security

stats.stat: Average healthcare data breach cost: USD 10.93M (2023), highest of any industry sector
stats.source: IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report, 2023

stats.stat: ISO 27001 certified organizations experience 65% fewer information security incidents than non-certified peers
HIPAA violations averaged USD 1.4M in penalties per case (2023); largest single penalty USD 49M
stats.source: HHS Office for Civil Rights HIPAA Enforcement Data

blocks.body:
Compliance requirements: What your organization must do

For HIPAA (US): If you use a digital falls assessment tool, you must have a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the vendor. The BAA specifies that the vendor will implement security safeguards and comply with HIPAA. Your organization is liable if the vendor breaches HIPAA, so vendor selection is critical. Request a copy of the vendor's security documentation, HIPAA BAA, and evidence of compliance (ISO 27001 certification, audit reports, etc.).

Your organization must also implement your own safeguards: workforce security (controlling which staff access patient data), access controls (ensuring staff access only data relevant to their role), security awareness training (teaching staff about passwords, phishing, etc.), and incident response procedures (defining what to do if a breach is discovered).

For GDPR (EU/UK): If you process personal data from EU/UK residents, GDPR applies. You must have a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with any vendor. The DPA specifies roles: you are the Data Controller (determining purposes and means of processing), the vendor is the Data Processor (processing data on your behalf). The vendor cannot use data for their own purposes.

You must implement privacy by design: security is built in from the start, not added later. Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) for high-risk processing. Implement data minimization: collect only necessary data. Implement purpose limitation: use data only for stated purposes. Document all processing in a Data Processing Register. Respond promptly to individuals exercising their rights (access, deletion, portability).

For both HIPAA and GDPR: Conduct vendor due diligence before contracting. Request security documentation, certifications, audit reports, and sample BAA/DPA. Understand vendor's data retention practices (how long they store data after you're done with it). Clarify vendor's responsibilities for security versus your organization's. Keep copies of all agreements and security documentation for audit purposes.

Responding to a data security incident

Despite best efforts, breaches can occur. Preparation is critical. All organizations should have an incident response plan. This plan specifies: (1) who is responsible for incident response (assign a coordinator), (2) how to detect a breach (monitoring for suspicious activity), (3) how to contain it (limiting further exposure), (4) how to notify affected individuals and regulators, (5) how to document lessons learned.

If you discover a breach (whether affecting Able Assess or your own systems), act quickly. Contain the breach: disconnect affected systems if necessary. Assess the scope: how many patients are affected? What data was exposed? Notify your vendor (Able Assess) to coordinate response. Contact your legal and compliance teams. Prepare notifications for affected individuals and regulators.

HIPAA requires notification to affected individuals within 60 days of discovery. GDPR requires notification to affected individuals and regulators within 72 hours of discovery. Delays in notification incur additional penalties. Notifications must explain what data was exposed, steps being taken to prevent future incidents, and what individuals can do to protect themselves.

After the incident, conduct a root-cause analysis. What went wrong? Why weren't existing controls effective? What changes can prevent recurrence? Implement improvements. Document all incident details for regulatory submissions and future reference.

items.question: Is HIPAA compliance guaranteed if a vendor has a BAA?
items.answer: A BAA is a contractual agreement, not a guarantee. The vendor commits to HIPAA compliance, but your organization is ultimately liable if the vendor breaches. Review the vendor's security practices, request evidence of compliance (ISO 27001 certification, audit reports), and audit the vendor regularly. A BAA is necessary but not sufficient.

items.question: What's the difference between encryption and hashing?
items.answer: Encryption is reversible: encrypted data can be decrypted with the correct key. Hashing is one-way: hashed data cannot be decrypted. For patient data, encryption is preferred because sometimes you need to access original data (e.g., to contact patients). Hashing is used for passwords: you hash the password, compare it to stored hash, but never store or transmit the original password.

items.question: How often should vendors update their security practices?
items.answer: Continuously. Security is not a one-time implementation but an ongoing process. Vendors should patch software vulnerabilities as soon as they're discovered (typically within 30 days), conduct regular penetration testing (annually minimum), maintain ISO 27001 certification (requires annual audits), and update security policies as threats evolve.

items.question: Can we audit a vendor's security practices ourselves?
items.answer: You can request security documentation (encryption methods, access controls, audit processes) and ask the vendor to explain their security architecture. However, full security audits (penetration testing, code review, infrastructure assessment) are technically complex. Rely on third-party certifications (ISO 27001) as evidence that independent experts have validated security.

items.question: What if a vendor says they're HIPAA-compliant but doesn't have ISO 27001 certification?
items.answer: ISO 27001 is not required for HIPAA compliance, but it demonstrates systematic security practices. If a vendor claims HIPAA compliance but lacks ISO 27001 or other third-party validation, ask for detailed security documentation and evidence of security testing. Be cautious if security practices are not transparently documented.

items.question: How do we ensure our EHR integration doesn't compromise data security?
items.answer: Ensure the API connection is encrypted (TLS 1.2+), authentication is mutual (both systems verify each other), and access control is enforced (only authorized systems and users can access data). Request that your EHR vendor and assessment vendor conduct security testing of the integration. Document the security architecture and review it before going live.

blocks.heading: Protect patient data with security-first falls assessment
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess is HIPAA-compliant, GDPR-aligned, and ISO 27001 certified. AES-256 encryption, TLS 1.2+ transit security, and role-based access control.
